subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Constitutional Checks and Balances vs. Social Movements in Representing the People's Will
stimulus: An important feature of a democratic republic is that it represents the will of the people. Take a position on whether constitutional checks and balances or citizen participation in social movements is more effective in ensuring the people's will is represented.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Article I of the United States Constitution, The Federalist 10, "Letter from a Birmingham Jail".)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents in the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Checks and balances are more effective in ensuring the people's will is represented because they guarantee that parts of the government that defy the people will be counteracted or removed entirely.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Social movements are more effective because they act independently of the government and can force the government to respond to the will of the people.

wrong_examples: History has shown that citizen participation in social movements ensures that the people's will is represented. (Makes a historical assertion but lacks a line of reasoning explaining why this is more effective than checks and balances)
Checks and balances are a feature of the Constitution. (States a fact about checks and balances without taking a position on their effectiveness in representing the people's will)
common_traps: The thesis must compare TWO methods and argue one is MORE effective — a thesis that describes only one method without comparing is insufficient
The prompt asks about representing the 'will of the people' specifically — thesis should connect to this concept, not just claim one method is generally better

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award the HIGHEST tier met. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A. Tier 3 requires at least one piece from Article I, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Article I (congressional powers), Federalist 10 (factions and representative government), Letter from Birmingham Jail, the Civil Rights Movement, or another relevant course concept about representation and democracy.

wrong_examples: Completely off-topic evidence with no connection to democratic representation or checks and balances
common_traps: Tier 1 does NOT require Row A — even without a thesis, a student can earn 1 evidence point
Inaccurate but topically relevant evidence can earn Tier 1 — accuracy is required at Tiers 2 and 3

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A.

required_elements: One specific piece that directly supports the thesis, OR two relevant pieces
alternatives.correct_example: Cites Federalist 10 to argue that representative government through checks and balances controls factions and ensures minority rights are not overrun by majority will — supporting the checks and balances thesis.

wrong_examples: Mentions the Letter from Birmingham Jail but describes it as arguing for legal challenges rather than social movements (mischaracterizes the document)
common_traps: The Letter from Birmingham Jail argues FOR peaceful civil disobedience as a moral obligation — it supports social movements, not legal challenges

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document (Article I, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail). Requires Row A.

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one from: Article I, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Article I's provisions for congressional oversight (checks and balances) to show how the legislative branch represents the people by limiting executive power, AND uses Federalist 10's argument about representative government controlling factions — both supporting the claim that checks and balances better represent the people's will.

wrong_examples: Two pieces of evidence but neither from Article I, Federalist 10, or Letter from Birmingham Jail
Cites Federalist 51 (not on the listed documents list) as the foundational document evidence — Federalist 51 is not listed for this FRQ
common_traps: Only Article I, Federalist 10, and the Letter from Birmingham Jail satisfy the foundational document requirement for this FRQ — Federalist 51, 70, or 78 do not qualify
Federalist 10 argues that a large republic with representative government (checks and balances built in) is better at controlling factions than a direct democracy

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain the logical connection between evidence and thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: In Letter from a Birmingham Jail, King explains that waiting for the government to check itself has led to greater suffering for African Americans — therefore, social movements increase pressure on policymakers to gain outcomes favorable to their cause in ways that formal government structures alone cannot.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Article I provides Congress with the power to override a presidential veto. This power can be very effective in representing the will of the people because it can stop a president who uses the veto for personal or partisan reasons rather than in response to the public's wishes.

wrong_examples: Letter from Birmingham Jail supports social movements. (States the connection but does not explain the causal or logical reasoning behind it)

Checks and balances were designed by the founders to protect democracy. (Historical statement without connecting the evidence to the specific thesis about which method is more effective at representing the people's will)
common_traps: Reasoning must explain the mechanism — WHY does the evidence lead to the conclusion that your chosen method is more effective at representing the people's will?
Requires Row A AND Row B ≥ 2 pts — Row C cannot be earned independently

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: While social movements are broadly based and can pressure the government to respond to what the people want, this is not the same as holding elected officials accountable to the people. Thus, social movements are more limited in what they can achieve, while checks and balances are able to protect against a much larger set of governmental abuses.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: While checks and balances are designed to hold the government accountable to the will of the people, they can also be used to slow or block popular policies. Because social movements are motivated by the people's direct desire to hold the government accountable, they are more immediately responsive to the people's will.

wrong_examples: Many would argue that social movements are better, but this is simply untrue. (Dismisses the alternate perspective without engaging substantively)
Some would argue that social movements are better because they can put extra pressure on government. (Only identifies the perspective — no rebuttal)
common_traps: The rebuttal must engage with the SUBSTANCE of the opposing argument — explaining why it fails or why your position is stronger
Requires Row A — without a thesis, Row D cannot be earned
